question: Given a static-only GitHub Pages site that must support asynchronous two-player gameplay with persistent shared state AND out-of-app push notifications when the opposing player has not opened the app, what is the simplest viable client-only architecture in 2026? Evaluate Firebase (Firestore + FCM Web Push), Supabase (Postgres + Edge Functions + Web Push), and a no-backend URL/code-payload model. For each: free-tier limits, client-side security model, true out-of-app push delivery support (especially iOS Safari Web Push status in 2026), and user setup burden.
constraints: GitHub Pages static-only hosting; no server-side execution available to the user
User should not be required to deploy code outside their GitHub Pages repo
Free-tier sufficient for a friends-and-family use case (two players, low-frequency play)
iOS Safari + Android Chrome + desktop browser support required
True out-of-app push (notification fires while recipient's tab is closed) is the test, not just in-tab toast
notes_on_coupling: IP1 (state transport) and IP2 (notification delivery) are explicitly flagged by Discovery as tightly coupled — choosing Firebase makes FCM the natural push partner; Supabase requires an Edge Function relay; a no-backend approach has no native push partner. Resolve as a pair.
